terms.definition: Retrieval-augmented generation: fetching relevant source material before asking a model to answer.
terms.interview_importance: Shows you can improve factuality without pretending the model itself knows private data.
terms.example: I would use RAG when answers must be grounded in current hotel policies.

terms.definition: The step that finds candidate documents, chunks, records, or tool outputs for an AI system.
terms.interview_importance: Many AI failures are retrieval failures, so senior PMs should diagnose this layer first.
terms.example: If the answer is wrong, I check whether retrieval brought back the right context.

terms.definition: A numeric representation of text, images, or other data used to compare semantic similarity.
terms.interview_importance: Helps explain semantic search, clustering, deduplication, and RAG trade-offs.
terms.example: We embedded support articles so similar questions map to nearby content.

terms.definition: A storage and search system optimized for nearest-neighbor lookup over embeddings.
terms.interview_importance: Useful when discussing retrieval scale, latency, freshness, and filtering.
terms.example: The vector database retrieves the closest policy chunks before generation.

terms.definition: A second scoring pass that reorders retrieved candidates for relevance or quality.
terms.interview_importance: Shows you know retrieval quality often improves after the first broad search.
terms.example: I would retrieve 50 chunks, then rerank down to the best 5.

terms.definition: Search that combines keyword matching with semantic/vector similarity.
terms.interview_importance: Good answer when exact terms, IDs, names, or semantic paraphrases all matter.
terms.example: Hybrid search catches both 'refund' and exact policy code references.

terms.definition: Search based on meaning rather than only exact keyword overlap.
terms.interview_importance: Lets you explain AI discovery and support workflows in non-technical language.
terms.example: A guest can ask 'late checkout' and still retrieve 'extended stay policy'.

terms.definition: A plausible model output that is unsupported, false, or fabricated.
terms.interview_importance: Interviewers expect concrete mitigation: grounding, evals, escalation, and monitoring.
terms.example: We route high-risk answers to fallback if grounding evidence is missing.

terms.definition: Tying model output to trusted source material, citations, tool results, or business rules.
terms.interview_importance: A strong AI PM answer distinguishes grounded answers from fluent guesses.
terms.example: The answer must cite the retrieved cancellation policy.

terms.definition: Rules, checks, UX constraints, or fallback paths that bound model behavior.
terms.interview_importance: Shows product ownership of risk instead of relying on prompts alone.
terms.example: A payment dispute bot needs escalation guardrails for high-value cases.

terms.definition: Model Context Protocol: a standard way for AI apps to expose tools and context to models.
terms.interview_importance: Mention only when relevant; it is an integration pattern, not a product strategy.
terms.example: MCP could expose CRM lookup as a controlled tool.

terms.definition: A system that can plan steps, call tools, observe results, and continue toward a goal.
terms.interview_importance: Good answers define autonomy level, tool scope, rollback, and human review.
terms.example: An agent may draft a refund response, but approval stays with support.

terms.definition: Using software or AI to complete repeated multi-step tasks with less manual effort.
terms.interview_importance: Frames AI value around time saved, quality, and operational throughput.
terms.example: Automating invoice triage matters if it reduces cycle time without raising errors.

terms.definition: A curated set of test cases used to measure model or system behavior.
terms.interview_importance: Shows you can make probabilistic quality measurable before launch.
terms.example: I would build an eval set from real support intents and edge cases.

terms.definition: A high-quality labeled evaluation set treated as the reference answer source.
terms.interview_importance: Useful for explaining release gates, regression tests, and quality thresholds.
terms.example: The golden set includes the expected answer and the source policy.

terms.definition: Using a model to score or compare outputs against a rubric.
terms.interview_importance: Strong answers mention calibration against human labels and known judge bias.
terms.example: I would use an LLM judge for scale, then audit it against human review.

terms.definition: The match between a system's confidence signal and the true likelihood it is correct.
terms.interview_importance: Important for deciding when to answer, defer, escalate, or ask clarification.
terms.example: High confidence should mean the answer is actually right most of the time.

terms.definition: Human-in-the-loop: a person reviews, approves, corrects, or handles uncertain cases.
terms.interview_importance: Shows realistic deployment thinking for high-risk workflows.
terms.example: HITL review handles refunds above a threshold until quality is proven.

terms.definition: The existing system, metric, or process used as the comparison point.
terms.interview_importance: Prevents overclaiming impact; every launch answer needs a before state.
terms.example: We compare the AI flow against the current support macro baseline.

terms.definition: The response time under which 95 percent of requests complete.
terms.interview_importance: Better than average latency when discussing real user experience and SLAs.
terms.example: The chatbot must keep p95 latency under the partner SLA.

terms.definition: The variable cost of model input and output tokens for each interaction.
terms.interview_importance: Shows AI PM maturity by tying quality decisions to unit economics.
terms.example: I would route easy intents to cheaper models to control token cost.

terms.definition: An experiment that compares variants across randomized user groups.
terms.interview_importance: Use when causality matters and the sample/risk profile supports experimentation.
terms.example: We A/B test checkout copy to measure conversion impact.

terms.definition: A group of users sharing a start time, behavior, plan, source, or other trait.
terms.interview_importance: Cohorts prevent blended averages from hiding retention or activation problems.
terms.example: Compare retained versus churned cohorts after first value.

terms.definition: The share of users or accounts that keep using the product over time.
terms.interview_importance: Often stronger than trial or demo usage for proving durable value.
terms.example: Weekly retained teams is better than one-off AI demo clicks.

terms.definition: The percentage of users who complete a desired action in a funnel.
terms.interview_importance: Useful for framing checkout, signup, onboarding, and activation problems.
terms.example: Direct booking conversion is the key hotel funnel metric.

terms.definition: A single primary metric intended to reflect delivered customer value and business growth.
terms.interview_importance: Strong PMs choose one but still protect against gaming with guardrails.
terms.example: Completed practice sessions with improved score could be a north-star metric.

terms.definition: A metric that must not worsen while optimizing the primary metric.
terms.interview_importance: Shows balanced product judgment around quality, trust, cost, and latency.
terms.example: Increase conversion while keeping refund rate and p95 latency within bounds.

terms.definition: Customer acquisition cost: the cost to acquire a customer or account.
terms.interview_importance: Critical for marketplace, SaaS, and direct-booking unit economics.
terms.example: AI must lift conversion enough to justify the hotel's CAC.

terms.definition: Lifetime value: expected gross profit or value from a customer over time.
terms.interview_importance: Pairs with CAC to judge whether growth is economically healthy.
terms.example: A direct guest relationship matters if it raises repeat booking LTV.

terms.definition: Product-market fit: strong evidence that a product solves an important market need.
terms.interview_importance: Avoid vague enthusiasm; cite retention, pull, expansion, or willingness to pay.
terms.example: PMF is not demo excitement; it is repeated use in a real workflow.

terms.definition: Learning work that clarifies user needs, risks, constraints, and opportunity size.
terms.interview_importance: Senior PM interviews reward evidence-seeking before solution commitment.
terms.example: I would interview operators before committing to an AI concierge.

terms.definition: Product requirements document describing problem, goals, scope, constraints, and acceptance criteria.
terms.interview_importance: For AI, a PRD should include evals, fallback, risk, and observability.
terms.example: The PRD defines acceptable factual error rate and escalation rules.

terms.definition: The smallest useful product version that tests the riskiest assumption.
terms.interview_importance: Shows you can reduce scope without losing the learning goal.
terms.example: Use a concierge MVP before building a full agent.

terms.definition: Return on investment: value gained compared with investment required.
terms.interview_importance: Tie AI work to revenue, cost reduction, risk reduction, or speed.
terms.example: The feature needs a credible ROI after inference and support costs.

terms.definition: A decision where improving one dimension costs another, such as quality versus latency.
terms.interview_importance: Senior answers name the trade-off and the decision rule.
terms.example: Use a slower model only for high-stakes intents.

terms.definition: The first moment a user experiences meaningful product value.
terms.interview_importance: Useful for diagnosing high signup but low retention.
terms.example: For PaperCoach, activation is completing a useful practice session.

terms.definition: Dividing users or accounts into meaningful groups for analysis or targeting.
terms.interview_importance: Prevents one-size-fits-all conclusions from weak data.
terms.example: Hotels may segment by property size, inbound mix, and direct-booking maturity.

terms.definition: Situation, Task, Action, Result: a structure for behavioral interview answers.
terms.interview_importance: Keeps examples concrete and prevents rambling.
terms.example: Use STAR for 'tell me about a time' questions.

terms.definition: An answer with a clear frame, ordered points, evidence, and a concise close.
terms.interview_importance: Signals senior communication under pressure.
terms.example: I would answer in three parts: risk, rollout, and metric.

terms.definition: Mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive: categories that avoid overlap and gaps.
terms.interview_importance: Helpful for case-style structuring, but do not sound robotic.
terms.example: Split launch risk into quality, latency, cost, and trust.

terms.definition: A short question asked before answering to pin down scope or assumptions.
terms.interview_importance: Shows judgment, but too many can look evasive.
terms.example: Before sizing ROI, I would ask whether we optimize revenue or cost.

terms.definition: The brief top-line answer before details.
terms.interview_importance: Useful with senior interviewers who want the point first.
terms.example: My short answer is build a narrow RAG pilot, not a broad agent.

terms.definition: A principled response to an interviewer pushback or objection.
terms.interview_importance: Turns challenge questions into evidence of judgment instead of defensiveness.
terms.example: If challenged on wrappers, defend workflow fit, data loop, and trust.

terms.definition: A constraint or uncertainty stated clearly so the answer does not overclaim.
terms.interview_importance: Especially important when using directional metrics or estimates.
terms.example: I would caveat that the retention lift is directional, not causal proof.
